title: Managing Weight Effects of Medications to Optimize Health in Patients With Overweight/Obesity
authors: Andrea D. Coviello, MD, MSc, MMCi, FACE

items.title: Why medication review is foundational in obesity care
items.text: Many common drug classes promote weight gain; polypharmacy can produce clinically meaningful adiposity when individually modest effects accumulate. Scan lists and substitute weight-neutral or weight-loss-promoting agents when feasible.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "A prudent approach is to scan medication lists for weight-promoting drugs and substitute weight-neutral or weight loss-promoting medications when feasible."

items.title: Obesity prevalence and comorbidity burden
items.text: Obesity (BMI ≥30) affects ~40% of Americans; another third are overweight. Obesity fuels prediabetes, type 2 diabetes, NAFLD, cardiovascular disease, reproductive disorders, and several cancers.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Obesity (BMI ≥30 kg/m²) now affects 40% of Americans and another third are overweight (BMI 25-29.9 kg/m²)."

items.title: How Endocrine Society obesity pharmacology guidance sequences care
items.text: Lifestyle modification is foundational; assess weight effects of current medications and switch to weight-neutral or weight-loss agents before adding antiobesity pharmacotherapy for appropriately selected patients.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "This includes assessment of weight effects of a patient's current medications with modification of the regimen to change weight gain-promoting medications to medications that promote weight loss or are weight neutral."

items.title: Why chronic glucocorticoids worsen obesity-related disease
items.text: Repeated or chronic steroids cause weight gain and central adiposity; obesity can then worsen the underlying condition (e.g., asthma). Use the least steroid necessary; prefer episodic treatment when possible.
items.reference: Glucocorticoids (Oral, Topical, Inhaled, Intra-articular Injections): "Steroid use can help control asthma, but weight gain and obesity can then make asthma worse."

items.title: How to choose diabetes medications for weight-conscious patients
items.text: Insulin and secretagogues promote weight gain; metformin is mildly weight-reducing; GLP-1 receptor agonists and SGLT-2 inhibitors promote weight loss and are preferred after metformin in many guidelines.
items.reference: Diabetes Medications: "most professional societies now recommend metformin as first-line treatment for type 2 diabetes followed by a GLP-1 receptor agonist or SGLT-2 inhibitor given their weight-loss effects."

items.title: Weight effects of antidepressants and antipsychotics
items.text: Not all psychotropics cause weight gain. Olanzapine causes the most (~2.4 kg in 3 months in trials, far more in practice). Metformin and topiramate may mitigate antipsychotic metabolic effects.
items.reference: Antidepressants and Antipsychotic Medications: "Olanzapine is associated with the most weight gain overall: 2.4 kg (5 lb) in 3 months in clinical trials, but frequently much more in clinical practice"

items.title: Why beta-adrenergic blockers can promote weight gain
items.text: Beta-blockers used for cardiovascular disease are among less-recognized weight-promoting classes. Propranolol for migraine prophylaxis may contribute to weight gain; topiramate is weight-losing and FDA approved for migraine.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "other classes of medication are less well recognized such as β-adrenergic blockers used to treat heart disease, heart rhythm disorders, and hypertension"

items.title: Antihistamines and appetite
items.text: Long-term antihistamine use is associated with weight gain in observational studies, likely via H1-receptor affinity increasing appetite. Adequate controlled quantitative data are limited.
items.reference: Antihistamines: "The weight gain-promoting effects of some antihistamines are thought to be mediated through their affinity for the H1-histamine receptor."

items.title: How semaglutide breaks the asthma–steroid–obesity cycle
items.text: In class III obesity with T2DM, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and steroid-exacerbated asthma, semaglutide improves glycemia and produces greater weight loss than SGLT-2 inhibitors or DPP-4 inhibitors.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: C) Semaglutide"

items.title: Why cariprazine may drive post-bariatric weight regain
items.text: After RYGB, weight regain with worsening diabetes on high-dose insulin may reflect atypical antipsychotic effects. Cariprazine causes weight gain; lisdexamfetamine and buspirone are not major contributors.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: A) Cariprazine"

items.title: How to switch migraine prophylaxis to promote weight loss
items.text: Rapid weight gain after starting/increasing propranolol in a euthyroid woman suggests beta-blocker effect. Topiramate is FDA approved for migraine prophylaxis, promotes weight loss, and requires reliable contraception due to teratogenicity.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: B) Switch propranolol to topiramate"

items.title: Weight effects of hormonal contraception and androgen therapy
items.text: Progesterone-only agents (depot MPA, levonorgestrel IUD) can promote adiposity in women. Testosterone therapy in hypogonadal men improves body composition; androgen deprivation for prostate cancer increases fat mass and metabolic risk.
items.reference: Reproductive Hormones (Hormonal contraceptives, Hormone Replacement Therapy with Sex Steroids): "agents can be particularly problematic for women and include progesterone-only formulations such as medroxyprogesterone acetate injections and levonorgestrel containing intrauterine devices."

items.question: A woman with class III obesity, T2DM (HbA1c 7.4%), hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and steroid-treated asthma needs a second diabetes agent. Best choice to lower glucose and promote weight loss?
items.explanation: GLP-1 RAs produce more weight loss (5–10%) than SGLT-2 inhibitors; DPP-4 inhibitors are weight neutral; pioglitazone promotes weight gain.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: C) Semaglutide"

items.question: Two years after RYGB, a woman regained 44 lb with HbA1c 10.6% on insulin glargine/lispro, liraglutide, metformin, lisdexamfetamine, buspirone, cariprazine, and fexofenadine. Which drug most likely contributes to regain?
items.explanation: Cariprazine is an atypical antipsychotic associated with weight gain; others listed are weight neutral or weight losing.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: A) Cariprazine"

items.question: A euthyroid woman gained >10% body weight after sertraline and escalating propranolol for migraines. Best next step?
items.explanation: Propranolol (beta-blocker) promotes weight gain; topiramate treats migraine and promotes weight loss—contraception required.
items.reference: Case 3: "Answer: B) Switch propranolol to topiramate"

items.question: Which agent may mitigate weight gain and insulin resistance from atypical antipsychotics?
items.explanation: Metformin ameliorates metabolic derangements from atypical antipsychotics and promotes mild weight loss.
items.reference: Diabetes Medications: "Metformin does ameliorate the metabolic derangements associated with newer psychotropic agents, particularly atypical antipsychotics."

items.question: Per the chapter, preferred sequencing after metformin for type 2 diabetes emphasizes:
options: GLP-1 receptor agonist or SGLT-2 inhibitor
items.explanation: Professional societies favor GLP-1 RAs or SGLT-2 inhibitors after metformin for weight-beneficial glycemic control.
items.reference: Diabetes Medications: "recommend metformin as first-line treatment for type 2 diabetes followed by a GLP-1 receptor agonist or SGLT-2 inhibitor"

items.question: Typical short-term trial weight gain with olanzapine cited in the chapter is approximately:
items.explanation: Trials show ~2.4 kg in 3 months; clinical practice weight gain can be far greater with prolonged use.
items.reference: Antidepressants and Antipsychotic Medications: "Olanzapine is associated with the most weight gain overall: 2.4 kg (5 lb) in 3 months in clinical trials"

items.question: Average weight loss with SGLT-2 inhibitors in type 2 diabetes is approximately:
items.explanation: SGLT-2 inhibitors typically produce 5–10 lb weight loss—less than injectable GLP-1 RAs.
items.reference: Diabetes Medications: "SGLT-2 inhibitors have also been associated with weight loss when used to lower glucose levels in type 2 diabetes, with typical weight loss of 5 to 10 lb (2.3 to 4.5 kg)."

items.question: Why is pioglitazone a poor choice when weight loss is a goal?
options: It stimulates adipocytes and promotes increased fat mass
items.explanation: TZDs including pioglitazone increase fat mass despite improving glycemia.
items.reference: Case 1: "Pioglitazone (Answer B) has been shown to improve fatty liver disease as well as glycemic control, but it promotes weight gain, not weight loss."

items.question: Long-term low-dose glucocorticoids for rheumatoid arthritis were associated with what weight change in a cited RCT?
items.explanation: Even low-dose chronic oral/IV steroids can produce 4–8% weight gain.
items.reference: Glucocorticoids (Oral, Topical, Inhaled, Intra-articular Injections): "long-term use of even low-dose glucocorticoids for rheumatoid arthritis in a randomized controlled trial was associated with a 4% to 8% weight gain."

items.question: How do DPP-4 inhibitors compare with GLP-1 receptor agonists for weight and glycemia?
options: Equal weight loss and glycemic effect
Less weight loss and less glycemic effect than GLP-1 RAs
Greater weight loss than GLP-1 RAs
items.explanation: DPP-4 inhibitors share the incretin pathway but lack significant weight loss and have less glycemic potency.
items.reference: Case 1: "The oral DPP-4 inhibitors such as sitagliptin (Answer A) work on the same pathway as GLP-1 receptor agonists, but they are not associated with significant weight loss and have less effect on glycemic control."

items.question: Before starting topiramate for migraine in a reproductive-age woman, what is essential?
items.explanation: Topiramate is teratogenic; reliable contraception is mandatory in reproductive-age women.
items.reference: Case 3: "As a reproductive-aged woman, it is important that she use reliable contraception if she were to start topiramate, as this drug can have teratogenic effects on a developing fetus."

items.question: Sertraline and escitalopram are categorized in the chapter as:
items.explanation: Both sertraline and escitalopram are considered weight neutral—switching between them will not aid weight loss.
items.reference: Case 3: "Both sertraline and escitalopram are considered to be weight neutral"

items.question: Which scenario poses the greatest long-term weight risk?
options: Years of atypical antipsychotic plus insulin for T2DM
items.explanation: Chronic medications for chronic diseases produce sustained adiposity exposure; brief steroids are less cumulative.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "The most problematic weight-promoting medications are those that are used to treat chronic diseases, which means the patient is exposed to weight-promoting medications over long periods."

items.statement: Multiple weight-neutral medications can never combine to cause clinically significant weight gain.
items.explanation: Polypharmacy with small individual effects can sum to greater weight gain.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Polypharmacy can also be a problem when a patient is prescribed multiple medications, each of which may have a relatively small individual effect, but which may result in greater weight gain when used concomitantly."

items.statement: Metformin is commonly associated with mild weight loss (~3%–4%) in most patients.
items.explanation: Metformin is not FDA approved for weight loss but commonly produces mild weight reduction.
items.reference: Diabetes Medications: "Although metformin is not FDA approved for weight loss, it is commonly associated with mild weight loss (3%-4%) in most patients"

items.statement: Chronic inhaled glucocorticoids cannot contribute to weight gain because they lack systemic absorption.
items.explanation: Inhaled/enteral steroids can suppress the HPA axis, implying sufficient systemic absorption to affect weight and metabolism.
items.reference: Glucocorticoids (Oral, Topical, Inhaled, Intra-articular Injections): "all have been implicated in secondary adrenal insufficiency. This suggests sufficient systemic absorption to suppress the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and therefore sufficient absorption to contribute to weight gain"

items.statement: Aripiprazole is always strictly weight neutral with no weight gain in any patient.
items.explanation: Aripiprazole is classified weight neutral but can cause ~9–10 lb gain and more in sensitive individuals.
items.reference: Case 2: "Some agents are classified as weight neutral, such as aripiprazole, but these agents are associated with mild weight gain of approximately 9 to 10 lb (4-4.5 kg) in general"

items.statement: Injectable GLP-1 receptor agonists are associated with more weight loss than oral SGLT-2 inhibitors.
items.explanation: GLP-1 RAs produce 5–10% body mass loss versus ~5–10 lb with SGLT-2 inhibitors.
items.reference: Case 1: "Injectable GLP-1 receptor agonists are associated with more weight loss (5%-10% of body mass) than the oral SGLT-2 inhibitors such as empagliflozin (Answer D)"

items.statement: Beta-adrenergic blockers are among medication classes associated with weight gain.
items.explanation: Beta-blockers for cardiovascular indications are less recognized but important weight-promoting agents.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "β-adrenergic blockers used to treat heart disease, heart rhythm disorders, and hypertension"

items.statement: Weight loss can improve asthma, type 2 diabetes, hypertension, NAFLD, and sleep apnea.
items.explanation: Multiple obesity-related conditions improve with weight reduction.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "Many other chronic conditions have been shown to improve with weight loss including type 2 diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia, metabolic syndrome, cardiovascular disease, osteoarthritis, nonalcoholic fatty liver disease, and sleep apnea."

items.statement: Topiramate may promote weight loss and improve glucose metabolism when used with antipsychotics.
items.explanation: Topiramate is an option beyond metformin for antipsychotic-associated weight/metabolic effects.
items.reference: Antidepressants and Antipsychotic Medications: "Topiramate, FDA approved for use in seizure disorders and migraine prophylaxis, has also been shown to promote weight loss and improve glucose metabolism when used in conjunction with antipsychotic medications."

items.assertion: Assertion: Reviewing medication lists for weight-promoting drugs is a first step in obesity treatment planning.
items.reason: Reason: All medications have identical effects on body weight.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; identical weight effects are false.
items.reference: Strategies for Diagnosis, Therapy, and/or Management: "One of the first steps in approaching a treatment plan for patients with overweight or obesity is to review their medication list"

items.assertion: Assertion: Obesity can worsen asthma after glucocorticoid-induced weight gain.
items.reason: Reason: Weight gain never affects pulmonary disease.
items.explanation: Assertion describes a vicious cycle; weight affecting asthma is false as stated.
items.reference: Glucocorticoids (Oral, Topical, Inhaled, Intra-articular Injections): "Steroid use can help control asthma, but weight gain and obesity can then make asthma worse."

items.assertion: Assertion: Semaglutide is preferred over sitagliptin when weight loss and glycemic improvement are both needed.
items.reason: Reason: DPP-4 inhibitors produce greater weight loss than GLP-1 receptor agonists.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; the reason reverses the weight effects of the drug classes.
items.reference: Case 1: "The oral DPP-4 inhibitors such as sitagliptin (Answer A) work on the same pathway as GLP-1 receptor agonists, but they are not associated with significant weight loss"

items.assertion: Assertion: Cariprazine may contribute to post-bariatric weight regain in Case 2.
items.reason: Reason: All atypical antipsychotics are strictly weight neutral.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; universal weight neutrality of antipsychotics is false.
items.reference: Case 2: "Cariprazine (Answer A) is known to cause weight gain"

items.assertion: Assertion: Propranolol may contribute to weight gain in Case 3.
items.reason: Reason: Beta-adrenergic blockers are associated with weight gain.
items.explanation: Both are true and consistent with the case timeline.
items.reference: Case 3: "Propranolol, a β-adrenergic blocker, is commonly used to reduce the frequency of migraines, but this medication class is associated with weight gain."

items.assertion: Assertion: High-dose insulin in type 2 diabetes can worsen insulin resistance via weight gain.
items.reason: Reason: Insulin is a catabolic hormone that promotes weight loss.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; insulin is anabolic and promotes weight gain—the reason is false.
items.reference: Diabetes Medications: "Insulin use in type 2 diabetes, which is strongly associated with overweight/obesity and insulin resistance, can be particularly detrimental."

items.assertion: Assertion: Long-term antihistamine use has been associated with weight gain in observational studies.
items.reason: Reason: Antihistamines decrease appetite via H1 receptor agonism.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; H1 antagonism increases appetite—the reason is false.
items.reference: Antihistamines: "Long-term use of antihistamines, including over-the-counter antihistamines, has been associated with weight gain in observational studies"

items.assertion: Assertion: Testosterone therapy in hypogonadal men can improve body composition.
items.reason: Reason: Testosterone increases adipose tissue and decreases lean mass.
items.explanation: Assertion is true; the reason describes the opposite of testosterone's effect on composition.
items.reference: Reproductive Hormones (Hormonal contraceptives, Hormone Replacement Therapy with Sex Steroids): "treatment with testosterone can improve body composition with loss of adipose tissue and gain of lean muscle mass."
